text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the right hemicolon laparoscopically, placing a gauze piece to create space and clearly expose the second part of the duodenum.

The surgeon elevates the mesentery while using gauze to push the duodenum downward, creating a retroperitoneal space. Carbon dioxide insufflation aids in preparing a clear retroperitoneal area without the use of an energy source.

The surgeon pushes the duodenum downwards while applying traction on the mesentery. A small incision is made below the right colic artery, and gauze is used for dissection. The third part of the duodenum becomes visible. Fat is removed from above the vessel, exposing the right colic artery, which arises from the superior mesenteric artery. Clips are placed, two proximally and one distally.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral abdominal wall, revealing the gonadal vessels and ureter. The ureter is positioned medial to the gonadal vessels, which are kept anterior during dissection. The colon is pushed upwards, and the ureter and gonadal vessels are observed turning downwards as dissection progresses to the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection by dividing the vessel. The procedure transitions to lateral to medial dissection, with the appendix and cecum held in medial traction.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make an incision in the lateral abdominal wall fold, aided by carbon dioxide insufflation for easier dissection. Continuous traction is applied to the cecum, facilitating a bloodless field. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure of the colon, with the colon being pushed medially and downwards.

The surgeon exposes the liver and gallbladder before proceeding to dissect the transverse colon. The transverse colon is separated from the mesocolon by applying upward traction on the mesocolon and pushing the colon downwards, resulting in complete division.

The surgeon separates the remnant of the transverse mesocolon from the colon. The right branch of the middle colic vein is clipped and divided using a harmonic device, followed by the division of the right branch of the middle colic artery.

The surgeon clips the transverse colon and ileum proximally and distally, then divides them in between to prepare for an intestinal anastomosis. A small incision is made to remove the specimen, and the anastomosis is performed extracorporeally.
